paper.title: Contextual Memory Virtualisation: DAG-Based State Management and Structurally Lossless Trimming for LLM Agents
paper.abstract: As large language models engage in extended reasoning tasks, they accumulate significant state -- architectural mappings, trade-off decisions, codebase conventions -- within the context window. This understanding is lost when sessions reach context limits and undergo lossy compaction. We propose Contextual Memory Virtualisation (CMV), a system that treats accumulated LLM understanding as version-controlled state. Borrowing from operating system virtual memory, CMV models session history as a Dir

memory_insight.compression_quantization_methods: DAG-based state management, structurally lossless trimming
memory_insight.before_after_comparisons: 20% reduction in token counts, up to 86% for sessions with significant overhead

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Implementing a three-pass structurally lossless trimming algorithm can reduce token counts by a mean of 20% and up to 86% for sessions with significant overhead.
technical_details: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) with formally defined snapshot, branch, and trim primitives
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Yes, but may require significant optimization for mobile devices with 4-8GB RAM due to high memory bandwidth requirements and peak DRAM usage.

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
Is the DRAM impact claim supported by benchmarks?
